title: Sort stack (O(nÂ²))
description: Sort stack using one stack (quadratic allowed)

examples.explanation: Original: [3,1,4,2] (top=2). Step1: pop 2, temp=[], push 2 to temp: temp=[2]. Step2: pop 4, temp=[2], 2<4, push 4: temp=[2,4]. Step3: pop 1, temp=[2,4], 4>1 so pop 4 to original: stack=[4], temp=[2]; 2>1 so pop 2 to original: stack=[4,2], temp=[]; push 1: temp=[1]. Step4: pop 2 from original, temp=[1], 1<2, push 2: temp=[1,2]. Step5: pop 4 from original, temp=[1,2], 2<4, push 4: temp=[1,2,4]. Step6: transfer temp to original: pop 4→stack=[4], pop 2→stack=[4,2], pop 1→stack=[4,2,1]. Reverse for top-first: original=[1,2,3,4] after re-indexing.
Original: [5]. Step1: pop 5, temp=[], push 5: temp=[5]. Step2: transfer temp to original: pop 5→stack=[5]. Done.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) Insertion Sort
approaches.summary: Use a temporary stack to hold sorted elements. Repeatedly pop from the original stack, insert each element into the temporary stack in sorted order by popping larger elements back to the original stack, then push the element. This mimics insertion sort on a stack.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Insertion Sort with One Temporary Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting a stack in descending order using insertion sort with one temporary stack.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack using only one additional temporary stack, with O(n²) time complexity allowed. The approach mimics insertion sort: we maintain the temporary stack in sorted order (descending from top to bottom) and insert each element from the original stack into its correct position. The intuition is that by repeatedly popping from the original stack and inserting into the temporary stack while maintaining sorted order, we gradually build a sorted stack. The algorithm works by: 1) While original stack is not empty, pop an element. 2) While temporary stack is not empty and its top is greater than the popped element, pop from temporary stack and push to original stack (to make space). 3) Push the popped element to temporary stack. 4) Repeat until original stack is empty. 5) Transfer all elements from temporary stack back to original stack to get ascending order from top. The time complexity is O(n²) because in worst case each element might cause popping all elements from temporary stack. Space complexity is O(n) for the temporary stack. This connects to L1 as it uses the insertion subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling empty stacks properly, incorrect comparison direction for sorting order, and infinite loops due to improper stack transfers. Real code pattern: def sort_stack(stack): temp = [] while stack: curr = stack.pop() while temp and temp[-1] > curr: stack.append(temp.pop()) temp.append(curr) while temp: stack.append(temp.pop()) return stack

examples.trace: Original: [3,1,4,2] (top=2). Step1: pop 2, temp=[], push 2 to temp: temp=[2]. Step2: pop 4, temp=[2], 2<4, push 4: temp=[2,4]. Step3: pop 1, temp=[2,4], 4>1 so pop 4 to original: stack=[4], temp=[2]; 2>1 so pop 2 to original: stack=[4,2], temp=[]; push 1: temp=[1]. Step4: pop 2 from original, temp=[1], 1<2, push 2: temp=[1,2]. Step5: pop 4 from original, temp=[1,2], 2<4, push 4: temp=[1,2,4]. Step6: transfer temp to original: pop 4→stack=[4], pop 2→stack=[4,2], pop 1→stack=[4,2,1]. Reverse for top-first: original=[1,2,3,4] after re-indexing.
Original: [5]. Step1: pop 5, temp=[], push 5: temp=[5]. Step2: transfer temp to original: pop 5→stack=[5]. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Stack
ladders.desc: Insert a single element into a sorted stack while maintaining sorted order using original stack as buffer.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the insertion sort approach: given a sorted stack (descending from top) and an element to insert, place the element in its correct position. The intuition is to use the original stack as temporary storage to pop larger elements from the sorted stack until we find the correct insertion point. The algorithm: 1) While sorted stack is not empty and its top is greater than the element to insert, pop from sorted stack and push to original stack. 2) Push the element to sorted stack. 3) While original stack is not empty, pop from original stack and push back to sorted stack. This ensures sorted order is maintained. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements in sorted stack, worst-case O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for using original stack as buffer. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly for each element. Common mistakes include not checking for empty stack, incorrect comparison (should be > for descending order), and forgetting to push back elements from buffer. Real code pattern: def insert_sorted(sorted_stack, element, buffer_stack): while sorted_stack and sorted_stack[-1] > element: buffer_stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()) sorted_stack.append(element) while buffer_stack: sorted_stack.append(buffer_stack.pop())

examples.trace: Start: sorted=[4,2,1], element=3, buffer=[]. Step1: top of sorted=1, 1<3, no pop. Step2: push 3 to sorted: sorted=[4,2,1,3]. Step3: buffer is empty, done. Actually correction: sorted is descending from top, so top=4. Step1: 4>3, pop 4 to buffer: sorted=[2,1], buffer=[4]. Step2: top=2, 2<3, stop. Step3: push 3: sorted=[2,1,3]. Step4: pop from buffer: pop 4 to sorted: sorted=[2,1,3,4]. Final sorted=[4,3,2,1] after re-indexing.
Start: sorted=[], element=5, buffer=[]. Step1: sorted empty, skip while. Step2: push 5: sorted=[5]. Step3: buffer empty, done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintain Sorted Order While Popping and Pushing Between Stacks
ladders.desc: The technique of using two stacks to maintain a sorted sequence by transferring elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the insight that by strategically popping and pushing between two stacks, we can keep one stack sorted. The intuition is that a stack's LIFO property can be used to reverse order temporarily, allowing insertion into sorted position. The algorithm: given stack A (unsorted) and stack B (sorted), to insert an element x: 1) While B is not empty and top of B > x, pop from B and push to A. 2) Push x to B. 3) While A is not empty (from step 1), pop from A and push back to B. This ensures B remains sorted in descending order from top. The key is that moving elements to A acts as a buffer to access deeper elements in B. Time complexity depends on number of elements moved, up to O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the stacks. This connects to L1 as it's the mechanism for the insertion subroutine. Common mistakes include infinite loops if not tracking which stack is which, and incorrect condition for stopping pops. Real code pattern: while stack_b and stack_b[-1] > x: stack_a.append(stack_b.pop()) stack_b.append(x) while stack_a: stack_b.append(stack_a.pop())

examples.trace: Start: stack_a=[], stack_b=[4,2,1], x=3. Step1: stack_b top=4, 4>3, pop 4 to stack_a: stack_a=[4], stack_b=[2,1]. Step2: stack_b top=2, 2<3, stop. Step3: push x=3 to stack_b: stack_b=[2,1,3]. Step4: while stack_a not empty: pop 4 from stack_a to stack_b: stack_b=[2,1,3,4]. Final stack_b=[4,3,2,1] after re-indexing.
Start: stack_a=[], stack_b=[], x=5. Step1: stack_b empty, skip. Step2: push 5 to stack_b: stack_b=[5]. Step3: stack_a empty, skip. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare and Transfer Elements Between Two Stacks
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of moving elements from one stack to another based on a condition.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental operation of transferring elements between two stacks while comparing them. The intuition is that by popping from one stack and pushing to another, we can inspect and rearrange elements. The algorithm: given stack A and stack B, and a condition (e.g., while top of B > value), repeatedly pop from B and push to A until condition is false. This allows accessing elements in B without losing them, as they are stored temporarily in A. Time complexity is O(m) where m is number of elements transferred. Space complexity is O(n) for the stacks. This connects to L2 as it's the key step in maintaining sorted order. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in condition, not handling empty stacks, and incorrect order of operations. Real code pattern: while stack_b and stack_b[-1] > value: stack_a.append(stack_b.pop())

examples.trace: Start: stack_a=[], stack_b=[5,3,1], value=2. Step1: stack_b top=5, 5>2, pop 5 to stack_a: stack_a=[5], stack_b=[3,1]. Step2: stack_b top=3, 3>2, pop 3 to stack_a: stack_a=[5,3], stack_b=[1]. Step3: stack_b top=1, 1<2, stop. Final: stack_a=[5,3], stack_b=[1].
Start: stack_a=[], stack_b=[], value=4. Step1: stack_b empty, condition false, skip. Final: stack_a=[], stack_b=[].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pop from One Stack and Push to Another
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of moving an element between two stacks.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves popping an element from the top of one stack and pushing it onto another stack. The intuition is that this transfers the element while preserving the LIFO order indirectly. The algorithm: if source stack is not empty, pop its top element and push it to destination stack. This is a single step that can be repeated to move multiple elements. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. This connects to L3 as it's the atomic action used in transfers. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack (causing error) or not updating stacks correctly. Real code pattern: if source_stack: element = source_stack.pop() destination_stack.append(element)

examples.trace: Start: source=[3,2,1], dest=[]. Step1: pop from source: element=1, source=[3,2]. Step2: push to dest: dest=[1]. Final: source=[3,2], dest=[1].
Start: source=[], dest=[4]. Step1: source empty, no pop. Final: source=[], dest=[4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Operations: Push and Pop
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite of adding and removing elements from a stack.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic stack operations: push (add an element to the top) and pop (remove the top element). A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure. The intuition is that like a stack of plates, you can only add or remove from the top. Push operation adds an element to the top, increasing stack size. Pop operation removes the top element and returns it, decreasing stack size. If the stack is empty, pop may cause an error. Time complexity for both is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. This connects to L4 as these operations are used to move elements between stacks. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack without check, or misunderstanding the order of elements. Real code pattern: stack = [] # initialize empty list as stack. stack.append(x) # push x to top. x = stack.pop() # pop top element.

examples.trace: Start: stack=[]. Step1: push 5: stack=[5]. Step2: pop: remove 5, stack=[], return 5.

examples.input: Push 3 to stack [1,2], then push 4, then pop
examples.trace: Start: stack=[1,2]. Step1: push 3: stack=[1,2,3]. Step2: push 4: stack=[1,2,3,4]. Step3: pop: remove 4, stack=[1,2,3], return 4.

testCases.input: Push 3 to stack [1,2], then push 4, then pop
Push 0 to stack [], then push 1, then pop
Push 2 to stack [5], then pop, then push 3
Push 9 to stack [8], then push 10, then pop, then pop
Push 6 to stack [], then push 7, then push 8, then pop
Push 100 to stack [99], then pop, then push 101

approaches.name: Recursive O(n²) Sort
approaches.summary: Use recursion to sort the stack. Recursively pop all elements, then insert each element back in sorted order by comparing with the top. This leverages the call stack as implicit extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using Recursion (O(n²))
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of sorting a stack using recursion with O(n²) time complexity.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack in ascending order (smallest at top) using only one additional stack implicitly through recursion. The recursive O(n²) approach works by leveraging the call stack to temporarily hold elements while sorting. The intuition is similar to insertion sort: we recursively remove all elements, then insert each element back into the stack in sorted order by comparing with the current top. At each recursive call, we pop an element, recursively sort the remaining stack, then insert the popped element at its correct sorted position. This creates a sorted stack from bottom to top. The time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case, each insertion might require popping all sorted elements (like insertion sort). Space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion call stack depth. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the sortedInsert helper function from L1 as its core component. Common mistakes include not handling base cases correctly in recursion, forgetting to return the stack after modifications, or incorrect comparison logic in insertion. The key insight is treating the recursion call stack as temporary storage while maintaining sorted order through careful insertion.

examples.trace: Initial stack: top->[3,1,4,2]. Call sortStack([3,1,4,2]): pop 3, recursively sort [1,4,2]. sortStack([1,4,2]): pop 1, recursively sort [4,2]. sortStack([4,2]): pop 4, recursively sort [2]. sortStack([2]): base case (size<=1), return [2]. Now insert 4 into sorted [2]: sortedInsert([2],4): 4>2, push 4, stack becomes [2,4]. Return [2,4]. Insert 1 into sorted [2,4]: sortedInsert([2,4],1): 1<2, pop 2, recursively insert 1 into [4]. sortedInsert([4],1): 1<4, pop 4, recursively insert 1 into []. sortedInsert([],1): push 1, return [1]. Push back 4: [1,4]. Push back 2: [1,2,4]. Return [1,2,4]. Insert 3 into sorted [1,2,4]: sortedInsert([1,2,4],3): 3>1, 3>2, 3<4, pop 4, recursively insert 3 into [1,2]. sortedInsert([1,2],3): 3>1, 3>2, push 3, stack [1,2,3]. Push back 4: [1,2,3,4]. Final sorted stack: top->[1,2,3,4].
Initial stack: top->[5]. Call sortStack([5]): size=1, base case, return [5]. No sorting needed for single element.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Stack
ladders.desc: Implement sortedInsert function that inserts an element into a sorted stack while maintaining order.
ladders.explanation: The sortedInsert function is the core helper that inserts a given element into a stack that is already sorted in ascending order (smallest at top). This is analogous to the insertion step in insertion sort. The intuition: since the stack is sorted, we can pop elements from the top until we find the correct position for the new element (where the top is smaller or equal to the element), then push the element, and finally push back all popped elements. This maintains the sorted property. The recursive implementation elegantly handles this: if the stack is empty or the element is greater than or equal to the top, we push it; otherwise, we pop the top, recursively insert the element into the remaining stack, then push back the popped top. This ensures the element finds its correct position deep in the stack. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (scanning entire stack), space is O(n) due to recursion. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main sortStack to insert each popped element. Common mistakes: incorrect comparison operators (should maintain ascending order), not pushing back popped elements, or infinite recursion if base case missing. The key is understanding that recursion naturally reverses and restores the stack order.

examples.trace: Initial sorted stack: top->[2,4], element=3. Call sortedInsert([2,4],3): top=2, 3>2, so push 3? No, need to check deeper. Actually algorithm: top=2, 3>2, but stack is [2,4] where 4 is below 2. Correct logic: if stack empty OR element >= top, push element. Here top=2, 3>=2 is true, so push 3: stack becomes [3,2,4]. Wait, this is wrong order! Let's trace properly: sortedInsert([2,4],3): stack not empty, top=2, element=3. Condition: if stack empty or element >= top: 3>=2 true, so push 3: stack becomes [3,2,4]. But [2,4] was sorted with 2 at top (smallest), 4 below. After push 3, top is 3, which is >2, so order broken. Actually the stack representation: top->[2,4] means 2 is on top, 4 is at bottom. After push 3, top->[3,2,4] means 3 on top, then 2, then 4. This is not sorted ascending (top should be smallest). I need to correct: The sorted stack should have smallest at top. For insertion, if element >= top, we push element on top, making it new top. But if element is larger than top, it should go below top? Let's rethink: For stack sorted ascending (smallest at top), to insert element x: if stack empty or x <= top, push x. Because if x is smaller or equal to current top, it should be new top. If x > top, we need to insert deeper. So condition should be if stack empty or x <= top. Let's retrace: sortedInsert([2,4],3): top=2, 3<=2 false, so else: pop top=2, call sortedInsert([4],3). sortedInsert([4],3): top=4, 3<=4 true, push 3: stack becomes [3,4]. Then push back popped 2: [2,3,4]. Now top->[2,3,4] is sorted ascending. Yes, that's correct.
Initial stack empty, element=5. Call sortedInsert([],5): stack empty, so push 5. Stack becomes [5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition for Sorting
ladders.desc: Understand how recursion can sort a stack by popping all elements and inserting back in order.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the recursive O(n²) sort is decomposing the problem into smaller subproblems using recursion. The intuition: to sort a stack, we can remove the top element, recursively sort the remaining stack, then insert the removed element into the now-sorted stack. This mirrors the divide-and-conquer strategy of recursion, where each recursive call handles a smaller stack. The key insight is that after the recursive call returns, the remaining stack is sorted, so inserting the popped element becomes straightforward using the sortedInsert function. This approach leverages the call stack to temporarily hold elements: when we pop an element before the recursive call, it's stored in the function's local variable; during recursion, multiple such variables are held in the call stack. The process continues until the base case of an empty or single-element stack (which is trivially sorted). Then, as recursion unwinds, each element is inserted into the progressively larger sorted stack. Time complexity arises from n recursive calls, each potentially calling sortedInsert which is O(n), giving O(n²). Space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic uses sortedInsert as the insertion mechanism. Common mistakes: not popping before recursion, incorrect base case, or misunderstanding the order of operations. The elegance lies in how recursion naturally inverts and restores the stack order.

examples.trace: Initial stack: top->[3,1]. Call sortStack([3,1]): pop top=3, recursively sort [1]. sortStack([1]): base case (size<=1), return [1]. Now insert 3 into sorted [1] using sortedInsert: sortedInsert([1],3): top=1, 3<=1 false, so pop 1, recursively insert 3 into []. sortedInsert([],3): push 3, return [3]. Push back popped 1: [1,3]. Return [1,3].
Initial stack: top->[2]. Call sortStack([2]): size=1, base case, return [2]. No recursion needed.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Stack Traversal and Element Holding
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse a stack recursively while holding elements in function calls.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on using recursion to traverse a stack and temporarily hold elements in the call stack. The intuition: recursion allows us to access stack elements in reverse order (from top to bottom) by popping in each call, with the popped value stored in the function's local variable or call stack frame. As we recurse deeper, we remove elements from the original stack, and they are 'remembered' in the recursion chain. When the base case is reached (empty stack), we start returning and can process the held elements in the reverse order of popping (which is the original order from bottom to top). This is crucial for sorting because we need to insert elements back in sorted order after the recursive sort. The step-by-step: define a recursive function that pops the top element, calls itself on the remaining stack, then processes the popped element (e.g., inserts it). The call stack implicitly acts as temporary storage. Time complexity for traversal is O(n) as each element is popped once; space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This traversal is the first half of the sort logic, before insertion. Common mistakes: forgetting to pop, not storing the popped value, or infinite recursion without base case. The key is understanding how recursion inverts order and how local variables persist across calls.

examples.output: Elements held in recursion: 1,2,3 in reverse order
examples.trace: Initial stack: top->[1,2,3]. Call traverse([1,2,3]): pop top=1, store in x, recursively traverse([2,3]). traverse([2,3]): pop top=2, store in x, recursively traverse([3]). traverse([3]): pop top=3, store in x, recursively traverse([]). traverse([]): base case, return. Now unwind: in traverse([3]) after recursive call, process x=3 (e.g., print or insert). Then in traverse([2,3]), process x=2. Then in traverse([1,2,3]), process x=1. So elements processed in order 3,2,1 (reverse of original pop order).
Initial empty stack. Call traverse([]): base case, return immediately. No elements held.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Recursion
ladders.desc: Master pushing and popping stack elements within recursive functions.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of pushing and popping on a stack, specifically within the context of recursive functions. The intuition: in recursion, each function call has its own stack frame where local variables (like a popped element) are stored. When we pop from the stack in a recursive call, we remove the top element and store it locally; this reduces the stack size for deeper calls. Later, when returning from recursion, we can push elements back onto the stack, potentially in a different order. This push-pop mechanism is essential for rearranging elements during sorting. The step-by-step: to pop, call stack.pop() and store the value; to push, call stack.push(value). In recursion, popping before a recursive call and pushing after it allows us to process elements in reverse order. Time complexity for each push/pop is O(1), but in recursion, they occur multiple times. Space complexity includes the recursion call stack. Connection to L3: These operations enable the traversal and holding of elements. Common mistakes: modifying the stack incorrectly (e.g., popping without storing, pushing at wrong time), or not handling empty stack cases. The key is to see how recursion couples with stack operations to manipulate data flow.

examples.trace: Start with empty stack. Push 5: stack becomes [5]. Pop: remove top, return 5, stack becomes [].

Initial stack: [2]. Define function f(stack): if stack not empty, pop top=2, store in x, recursively f([]). After recursive call, push x back: push 2, stack becomes [2]. Then call f with push 1: start stack [2], pop top=2, store, f([]), then push 2 back, then push 1? Let's do full example: function reverse(stack): if not empty: top = stack.pop(), reverse(stack), insertAtBottom(stack,top). But for simplicity, trace: stack initially [2]. Call op(stack): pop top=2, store in x, call op([]) which does nothing, then push x back: stack becomes [2]. Now if we call op([2]) with initial push 1? Actually, to show push after pop in recursion: define test(stack, val): stack.push(val), if val>0: pop top, store, recursively test(stack, val-1), push stored back. For val=1: push 1, stack [1]. pop top=1, store, recursively test([],0) which pushes 0? This is messy. Let's do a clear trace: function demo(stack): if stack not empty: x = stack.pop(), demo(stack), stack.push(x). Start with stack [1,2]. demo([1,2]): pop x=2, demo([1]). demo([1]): pop x=1, demo([]). demo([]): return. Then push x=1: stack becomes [1]. Then push x=2: stack becomes [1,2]. So stack unchanged.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stacks and Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Learn the fundamental concepts of stack data structure and recursive function calls.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces two key concepts: stacks and recursion. A stack is a linear data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order, like a pile of plates. Basic operations include push (add to top) and pop (remove from top). Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. Each recursive call creates a new stack frame in memory, storing local variables and return address. The intuition for combining them: recursion naturally uses a call stack, so when we manipulate a data stack recursively, we have two stacks interacting—the data stack and the call stack. This is powerful for algorithms like sorting, as the call stack can temporarily hold data stack elements. For beginners, understand that recursion requires a base case to stop infinite calls, and each call works on a smaller problem. Time and space complexity depend on the depth of recursion. Connection to L4: These basics enable the push/pop operations within recursive functions. Common mistakes: not defining base case, misunderstanding LIFO order, or confusing data stack with call stack. Start with simple examples to build intuition.

examples.trace: Start empty. Push 1: stack becomes [1]. Push 2: stack becomes [2,1] (2 on top). Push 3: stack becomes [3,2,1]. Top is 3.

examples.input: Recursive function to compute factorial of 3
examples.trace: fact(3): calls fact(2). fact(2): calls fact(1). fact(1): base case returns 1. fact(2) returns 2*1=2. fact(3) returns 3*2=6.

testCases.input: Recursive function to find max depth

approaches.name: Optimized Two-Stack Merge O(n log n) Not Allowed
approaches.summary: This approach is not applicable here because quadratic time is allowed, but it would involve using two stacks to simulate merge sort for O(n log n) time. However, the problem restricts to quadratic solutions, so it's mentioned as a theoretical alternative.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Stack Using One Stack (Quadratic Time)
ladders.desc: Complete solution to sort a stack using only one additional stack in O(n²) time
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a stack in ascending order (smallest at top) using only one additional temporary stack, with quadratic time complexity allowed. The intuition is to simulate insertion sort using two stacks: we'll repeatedly pop elements from the original stack and insert them into the temporary stack in sorted order. The key insight is that we can use the temporary stack to hold elements in reverse sorted order (largest at top), then transfer them back to the original stack to get ascending order. The algorithm works by: 1) Create an empty temporary stack. 2) While original stack is not empty, pop the top element. 3) While temporary stack is not empty AND the popped element is smaller than the top of temporary stack, pop from temporary stack and push back to original stack (this maintains the reverse sorted order in temp stack). 4) Push the popped element to temporary stack. 5) After processing all elements, transfer everything from temporary stack back to original stack. This takes O(n²) time because in worst case, for each element we might transfer all elements between stacks. Space is O(n) for the temporary stack. This connects to L1's insertion subroutine as the complete implementation. Common mistakes include: forgetting to transfer back to original stack, incorrect comparison direction, or infinite loops from improper stack operations. Real code pattern: def sort_stack(stack): temp = []; while stack: curr = stack.pop(); while temp and temp[-1] > curr: stack.append(temp.pop()); temp.append(curr); while temp: stack.append(temp.pop()); return stack

examples.trace: Original: [3,1,4,2] (top=3). Step1: pop 3, temp=[], push 3 to temp → temp=[3]. Step2: pop 1, temp=[3], 1<3 so pop 3 back to original → original=[3], push 1 to temp → temp=[1]. Step3: pop 3, temp=[1], 3>1 so push 3 to temp → temp=[1,3]. Step4: pop 2, temp=[1,3], 2<3 so pop 3 back to original → original=[3], 2>1 so push 2 to temp → temp=[1,2]. Step5: pop 3, temp=[1,2], 3>2 so push 3 to temp → temp=[1,2,3]. Step6: pop 4, temp=[1,2,3], 4>3 so push 4 to temp → temp=[1,2,3,4]. Transfer back: pop all from temp to original → original=[1,2,3,4].

Original: [5,2,7,1,3]. Process: pop 5 → temp=[5]. pop 2 → 2<5, move 5 back, push 2 → temp=[2], original=[5]. pop 5 → 5>2, push 5 → temp=[2,5]. pop 7 → 7>5, push 7 → temp=[2,5,7]. pop 1 → 1<7, move 7 back; 1<5, move 5 back; 1<2, move 2 back; push 1 → temp=[1], original=[2,5,7]. pop 2 → 2>1, push 2 → temp=[1,2]. pop 5 → 5>2, push 5 → temp=[1,2,5]. pop 7 → 7>5, push 7 → temp=[1,2,5,7]. pop 3 → 3<7, move 7 back; 3<5, move 5 back; 3>2, push 3 → temp=[1,2,3], original=[5,7]. pop 5 → 5>3, push 5 → temp=[1,2,3,5]. pop 7 → 7>5, push 7 → temp=[1,2,3,5,7]. Transfer back → original=[1,2,3,5,7].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Insert Element into Sorted Stack
ladders.desc: Insert a single element into a stack that's maintained in sorted order
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation of the full solution: given a sorted stack (in reverse order with largest at top) and a new element, insert the element at the correct position while maintaining the sorted order. The intuition is similar to insertion sort: we need to find the right spot for the new element by temporarily removing larger elements, placing the new element, then putting the larger elements back on top. The algorithm: 1) While the sorted stack is not empty AND the new element is smaller than the top element of the sorted stack, pop from sorted stack and push to a temporary holder (which will be the original stack in the full solution). 2) Push the new element onto the sorted stack. 3) While the temporary holder has elements, pop from it and push back to sorted stack. This ensures the sorted stack remains in reverse sorted order. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements in the sorted stack, as we might move all elements. Space is O(1) extra beyond the stacks. This connects to L0 as the repeated application of this subroutine sorts the entire stack. Common mistakes include: incorrect comparison direction (should compare new element with top), forgetting to restore elements from temporary holder, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: def insert_sorted(sorted_stack, element): while sorted_stack and sorted_stack[-1] > element: # Move larger elements out original_stack.append(sorted_stack.pop()); sorted_stack.append(element); while original_stack: sorted_stack.append(original_stack.pop())

examples.trace: Start: sorted=[5,3,1], element=2. Step1: 2<5? Yes, pop 5 to original → original=[5], sorted=[3,1]. Step2: 2<3? Yes, pop 3 to original → original=[5,3], sorted=[1]. Step3: 2<1? No, push 2 to sorted → sorted=[1,2]. Step4: restore from original: pop 3 → sorted=[1,2,3]; pop 5 → sorted=[1,2,3,5]. Result: [5,3,2,1] (top=5).
Start: sorted=[4,2], element=6. Step1: 6<4? No, push 6 to sorted → sorted=[2,4,6]. No elements moved. Result: [6,4,2] (top=6).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Reverse Sorted Order in Temporary Stack
ladders.desc: The technique of keeping elements in reverse sorted order in the temporary stack to enable efficient insertion
ladders.explanation: The central insight of the quadratic sorting approach is maintaining the temporary stack in reverse sorted order: largest element at the top, smallest at the bottom. This allows us to insert new elements efficiently by comparing with the top. The intuition: if we keep the temporary stack sorted in descending order (from top to bottom), then when we want to insert a new element, we only need to look at the top elements until we find one smaller than or equal to our new element. This works because in a stack, we can only access the top element, so having the largest at the top means we encounter the most restrictive comparison first. The algorithm for maintaining this: when inserting element x into temporary stack T, pop from T while T is not empty and top(T) > x, pushing those popped elements to the original stack (which acts as temporary storage). Then push x onto T, then restore the popped elements from original stack back to T. This ensures T remains in reverse sorted order. Time complexity for one insertion is O(k) where k is the size of T. Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L1 as the implementation detail of the insertion subroutine. Common mistakes include: reversing the comparison (using < instead of >), not restoring elements properly, or misunderstanding the order. Real code pattern: while temp and temp[-1] > new_element: original.push(temp.pop()); temp.push(new_element); while original: temp.push(original.pop())

examples.trace: Start: temp=[8,5,3], new=6. Step1: 8>6? Yes, pop 8 to original → original=[8], temp=[5,3]. Step2: 5>6? No, push 6 to temp → temp=[3,5,6]. Step3: restore 8 from original → temp=[3,5,6,8]. Result: [8,6,5,3] (top=8).

Start: temp=[4,2], new=1. Step1: 4>1? Yes, pop 4 to original → original=[4], temp=[2]. Step2: 2>1? Yes, pop 2 to original → original=[4,2], temp=[]. Step3: push 1 to temp → temp=[1]. Step4: restore: pop 2 → temp=[1,2]; pop 4 → temp=[1,2,4]. Result: [4,2,1] (top=4).

ladders.title: Building Block — Transfer Elements Between Stacks with Condition
ladders.desc: Move elements from one stack to another based on a comparison condition
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves transferring elements between two stacks while a certain condition holds, which is fundamental to the sorting algorithm. The intuition: we need to temporarily move elements out of the way to make space for a new element, then put them back. Specifically, we move elements from stack A to stack B while the top of A satisfies some condition (e.g., is greater than a value). The algorithm: 1) While stack A is not empty AND condition(top(A)) is true, pop from A and push to B. 2) Perform some operation (like pushing a new element). 3) While stack B is not empty, pop from B and push back to A. This pattern appears twice in the full solution: when inserting into the temporary stack, and when transferring back at the end. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of elements transferred. Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L2 as the mechanism for maintaining reverse sorted order. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check stack emptiness before accessing top, incorrect condition logic, or not restoring all elements. Real code pattern: while stack_a and condition(stack_a[-1]): stack_b.append(stack_a.pop()); # Do operation; while stack_b: stack_a.append(stack_b.pop())

examples.input: Stack A: [5,3,1], condition: element > 2, Stack B: []
examples.trace: Start: A=[5,3,1], B=[], condition: >2. Step1: A top=5>2? Yes, pop 5 to B → A=[3,1], B=[5]. Step2: A top=3>2? Yes, pop 3 to B → A=[1], B=[5,3]. Step3: A top=1>2? No, push 2 to A → A=[1,2]. Step4: restore from B: pop 3 → A=[1,2,3]; pop 5 → A=[1,2,3,5]. Result: A=[1,2,3,5], B=[].

examples.input: Stack A: [4,2], condition: element < 3, Stack B: []
examples.trace: Start: A=[4,2], B=[], condition: <3. Step1: A top=2<3? Yes, pop 2 to B → A=[4], B=[2]. Step2: A top=4<3? No, push 1 to A → A=[4,1]. Step3: restore from B: pop 2 → A=[4,1,2]. Result: A=[4,2,1], B=[].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations combined with comparing top element to a value
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using stack push and pop while checking the top element against a condition, which is the building block for the sorting algorithm. The intuition: stacks are LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structures, so we can only access the top element. To insert an element in sorted order, we need to repeatedly check the top and decide whether to pop it. The algorithm: 1) Check if stack is empty; if not, peek at the top element. 2) Compare the top element with a given value using a relational operator (>, <, etc.). 3) Based on the comparison, either pop the top element or push a new element. This is done in a loop to handle multiple elements. Time complexity per operation is O(1) for push/pop/peek. Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L3 as the repeated application of this operation enables conditional transfers. Common mistakes include: accessing top of empty stack, not updating stack state correctly, or confusion between peek and pop. Real code pattern: while stack and stack[-1] > value: # Compare top; stack.pop() # Remove top; stack.append(new_value) # Push new

examples.input: Stack: [5,3,1], compare top > 2, pop if true, then push 4
examples.trace: Start: stack=[5,3,1]. Step1: top=1>2? No, so don't pop. Push 4 → stack=[5,3,1,4]. But note: algorithm typically pops while condition holds, then pushes. Correct trace: while stack and top>2: pop. Here top=1 not >2, so no pop. Push 4 → stack=[5,3,1,4]. However, for sorting context, we might pop all >2 first. Let's redo: condition: pop while top>2. top=1>2? No, so stop. Push 4 → stack=[5,3,1,4]. But this isn't sorted. For learning, simpler example: stack=[4,2], compare top>3, pop if true. top=2>3? No, so no pop. Result: [4,2].

examples.input: Stack: [6,4], compare top > 5, pop if true, then push 7
examples.trace: Start: stack=[6,4]. Step1: top=4>5? No, so no pop. Push 7 → stack=[6,4,7]. But to show popping: if condition was top<5, pop while true. top=4<5? Yes, pop 4 → stack=[6]. top=6<5? No, stop. Then push 7 → stack=[6,7]. Result: [6,7].

testCases.input: Stack: [3,1], compare top > 0, pop if true, push 2
Stack: [5,2], compare top < 4, pop if true, push 3
Stack: [4], compare top > 3, pop if true, push 5
Stack: [7,5], compare top >= 6, pop if true, push 6
Stack: [], compare top > 1, pop if true, push 2
Stack: [6,4,2], compare top > 3, pop if true, push 5
Stack: [3,2,1], compare top < 2, pop if true, push 0
Stack: [8,6], compare top > 7, pop if true, push 9
Stack: [5,5,3], compare top >= 5, pop if true, push 4
Stack: [4,2], compare top > 1, pop if true, push 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Basics and Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Understanding stack operations and comparing numerical values
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: knowing how a stack works and how to compare elements. A stack is a data structure that follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out): you can push (add) an element to the top, pop (remove) the top element, and peek (view) the top element without removing it. In Python, a list can simulate a stack with append() for push, pop() for pop, and [-1] for peek. Comparing elements involves using relational operators like >, <, >=, <=, == to check if one value is greater than, less than, etc., another. For example, 5 > 3 is True, 2 < 1 is False. The intuition for the sorting problem builds on this: we need to compare the top of the stack with new elements to decide where to insert them. Time and space are O(1) for basic operations. This connects to L4 as the foundation for implementing push/pop with conditions. Common mistakes include: confusing stack order (top vs bottom), off-by-one errors in indices, or incorrect comparison semantics. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(1) # push; top = stack[-1] # peek; if top > 2: stack.pop() # pop;

examples.trace: Start: stack=[3,1]. Step1: push 2 → stack=[3,1,2]. Step2: peek top = 2. Step3: compare 2 > 1 → True. No pop in this example, just comparison.

examples.input: Stack: [4], pop, then push 5, compare new top < 6
examples.trace: Start: stack=[4]. Step1: pop → stack=[], removed 4. Step2: push 5 → stack=[5]. Step3: peek top = 5. Step4: compare 5 < 6 → True.

testCases.input: Stack: [3,2,1], pop, then pop, compare old second top > 0
Stack: [1,2], pop, push 0, compare new top < 1
